title: Oxtail stew
subtitle: Jamaican braised oxtail with butter beans
excerpt: Oxtail sections browned deeply and braised for three to four hours in a dark, fragrant sauce of browning, allspice, scotch bonnet, and thyme until the collagen has fully dissolved and the meat slides from the bone. Butter beans added in the final hour provide substance and absorb the rich braising liquid. The definitive Jamaican Sunday pot.
sourceNotes: Oxtail braised in the Jamaican style is documented in Caribbean cookbooks from the mid-20th century and draws on a tradition of slow-cooking economical cuts that runs through the entire Caribbean culinary tradition. The use of browning sauce for colour and the allspice-scotch bonnet base distinguish the Jamaican version from British or European oxtail soups.

content.text: Oxtail requires a proper low simmer for the full cook time. At a rolling boil the connective tissue toughens and the meat dries out; at barely a simmer the collagen dissolves slowly into the liquid and produces the thick, gelatinous sauce that defines the dish. Plan for 3 to 4 hours and do not try to rush it.

Browning sauce gives Jamaican oxtail its characteristic dark colour. It is not a seasoning, only 1 teaspoon goes into the marinade. If browning sauce is unavailable, dark soy sauce is the nearest substitute for colour, though the flavour differs slightly.

Rub the {{oxtail}} with the {{browning-sauce}}, {{allspice}}, {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{thyme}}, {{spring-onion}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Leave for at least 30 minutes. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large heavy pot over high heat. Brown the oxtail pieces in batches, 3 to 4 minutes per side, until deep brown all over. Remove and set aside.

Add the {{onion}} to the pot and cook for 5 minutes until golden. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and stir, scraping up the browned bits. Return all the oxtail to the pot with the {{scotch-bonnet}} (whole). Add enough water to just cover the meat (about 750 ml). Bring to a boil, then reduce to the lowest possible simmer. Cover and cook for 2 hours 30 minutes. Add the {{butter-beans}} and cook uncovered for a further 45 minutes, until the meat is falling from the bone and the sauce has reduced to a thick gravy. Remove and discard the scotch bonnet. Rest for 15 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Continue cooking. Some oxtail takes up to 4 hours. The liquid should barely tremble
items.cause: Simmer too vigorous or oxtail pieces too large
items.symptom: Meat not falling from the bone at 3 hours

items.fix: Remove the lid for the last hour and increase the heat slightly to reduce
items.cause: Too much water or lid kept on throughout

items.fix: Let the stew cool fully. Chill overnight. Lift off the set fat layer before reheating.
items.cause: Oxtail not browned sufficiently or too much fat on the pieces

content.text: Oxtail stew is the centrepiece of the Jamaican Sunday table, a dish made in the morning to be ready by afternoon. It requires no attention once the braise is started, the pot does the work. In the Jamaican diaspora in Britain it appears at Caribbean restaurants and as a home-cooking staple that connects to the Sunday meal culture of Jamaica. The allspice, browning sauce, and scotch bonnet give it a flavour profile that is distinctly Jamaican: warming, slightly sweet, and deeply savoury.
